EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm in a very moist monsoonal airmass produced a funnel cloud over the Uncompahgre Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This funnel cloud was observed by mobile Skywarn spotters who were near the funnel cloud, as well as some NWS employees who were at the Grand Junction forecast office.  Several photographs were taken of the funnel cloud that emanated from a classic wall cloud.

A strong, slow-moving upper low pressure area moved across eastern Colorado and western Kansas during the afternoon and early evening hours.  A round of severe weather occurred with thunderstorms initially producing large, damaging hail.  By late afternoon, a NE-SW line of supercells developed and moved northeast across Cheyenne and Kit Carson counties, producing numerous tornadoes near the town of Kit Carson in Cheyenne county. Later in the evening, storms produced flash flooding over portions of western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed southwest of Kit Carson and dissipated just west of town.  No known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest surface gradient winds developed during the morning hours and continued through most of the day across eastern Colorado, southwest Nebraska and western Kansas. Peak wind gusts of 50 to 70 mph were common across the area, producing minor damage, including a broken utility pole, and tree and awning damage.  One vehicle was blown over on Interstate 70 near Colby, Kansas but no injuries occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was recorded at Firstview, just west of Cheyenne Wells.  Another gust to 59 mph was reported at a mesonet site 9 miles NNE of Kit Carson at the home of a cooperative observer.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two skiers were caught in an avalanche along Dry Gulch, which is east of the Eisenhower Tunnel.  One skier was killed when he was buried under 2 to 3 feet of snow; the other suffered minor injuries.  It took approximately 15 minutes for the injured skier to locate his friend, and another 15 minutes to excavate him and initiate CPR.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler was injured when he triggered an avalanche near Jones Pass, in near whiteout conditions.  Visibilities at the time ranged from 10 feet to 100 feet.  He was caught and buried in the slide that was approximately 100 feet long.  While in the avalanche, he discharged his airbag which helped him to float to the surface.  The snowmobiler was partially buried in approximately 6 to 18 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped out of British Columbia and into the Central Rockies producing heavy snow over many western Colorado mountain locations, as well as significant snowfall in some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. The heaviest accumulations favored the Uncompahgre Gorge on the northern side of the San Juan mountains, where nearly 30 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall produced accumulations ranging from 8 to 13 inches. Wind gusts to 30 mph produced areas of blowing snow.

A snowmobiler was caught in an avalanche near Rabbit Ears Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two snowmobilers were riding in an area about a half mile west of Rabbit Ears Pass.  One of the snowmobilers went up an untracked slope to make the first highmark.  The second snowmobiler started up shortly thereafter and got stuck before the first snowmobiler was off the slope. The first snowmobiler then turned and was sidehilling over to the second snowmobiler's location when the avalanche released. The avalanche caught and partially buried the second snowmobiler.  No injuries or damage were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into the Great Basin Region pulling a strong cold front with it. The front stalled over northeast Utah, where it produced heavy snowfall over the eastern Utah mountains. The front then pushed through western Colorado spreading heavy snow over the Colorado mountains and higher valleys across northwest Colorado. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall produced accumulations ranging from 12 to 22 inches. Winds gusted up to 50 mph generating extensive blowing and drifting snow. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier was caught in an avalanche in Picayne Gulch northeast of Silverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier triggered a slab avalanche in Picayne Gulch about 9 miles northeast of Silverton.  The skier was 10-15 feet below the fracture line when the avalanche started.  The skier was carried about 150 yards and was buried to his neck.  Two other skiers descended the slope and extricated the victim within about 10 minutes.  The avalanche also ran across San Juan County Road 2 at the base of the slope.  No injuries were reported.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two skiers headed up Castle Peak in the Elk Mountains when they encountered an avalanche that carried one skier downhill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two skiers climbed up the east face of Castle Peak during the late morning hours of the 24th. After performing several snow stability tests, the skiers saw no potential avalanche risk. Skier one was the first to descend the mountain when a slab released above him and carried him downhill. He was just coming to a stopping point when another slab released and hit him in the back, which propelled him even further downhill. The skier was carried about 750 feet, but managed to stay upright and on top of the snow surface. The skier was buried up to his boots but managed to ski out with only a minor leg injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow-moving upper low pressure area moved across eastern Colorado and western Kansas during the afternoon and early evening hours.  A round of severe weather occurred with thunderstorms initially producing large, damaging hail.  By late afternoon, a NE-SW line of supercells developed and moved northeast across Cheyenne and Kit Carson counties, producing numerous tornadoes near the town of Kit Carson in Cheyenne county. Later in the evening, storms produced flash flooding over portions of western Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado remained over open, rural portions of the county with no known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of light snow, blowing snow, black ice and careless driving resulted in a 34-vehicle crash along Interstate 25 near Monument.  Five tractor-trailers were involved in the pileup.  Although there were no fatalities, 18 people were seriously injured and taken to nearby hospitals.  The northbound lanes of the interstate were closed for several hours, snarling traffic between Denver and Colorado Springs.

At the same time a surface dry line lingered over western Kansas before moving rapidly eastward, followed by a cold front. In western Kansas, numerous tornadoes developed along the dry line just after noon, with one destroying a small shed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water to a depth of one foot was seen on roadways around the Catamount Reservoirs and Crystal Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough extending from an upper low in southern Canada brushed the region with significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 1 to 3 inches with locally higher amounts up to 5 inches occurred across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough extending from an upper low in southern Canada brushed the region with significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall of 1 to 3 inches with locally higher amounts up to 6 inches occurred across the zone.  Numerous vehicle accidents occurred in the Blue Mesa and Cerro Summit areas of Highway 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough extending from an upper low in southern Canada brushed the region with significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short-lived snowfall event produced 2 to 4 inches of snowfall.  Wind gust of 40 to 60 mph produced local blizzard conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occurred across the zone. Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds resulted in a controlled burn to become a wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A controlled burn at a farm became a wildfire when breezy winds developed and blew embers beyond the planned burn area.  A 100 foot by 50 foot area of baled hay burned up, and a number of vehicles and farm implements were destroyed.  Winds gusts in the west-central valleys of Colorado were measured at 20 to 25 mph.  A firefighter incurred minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brushed the mountains of southwest Colorado with significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches occurred above the 9000 foot level.  Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph resulted in local areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered intense thunderstorms produced produced a variety of severe weather they tracked toward the southeast during the late afternoon into the early evening hours. Hail over four inches in diameter, gusts to 70 mph and flash flooding resulted from the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel sized hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved from northeast Colorado into extreme northern Kansas during the late evening hours, producing wind gusts as high as 81 mph and golf ball sized hail.  some tree limb and power pole damage occurred.
A thunderstorm collapse resulted in a broad area of outflow wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong eastward-moving thunderstorm cell collapsed over central Delta County and produced damaging winds across portions of eastern Delta County.  Measured wind speeds included 79 mph at a location 4 miles northwest of Hotchkiss and 90 mph at a location just under 3 miles southeast of Hotchkiss.  The damage pach extended from about 5 miles west-northwest of Hotchkiss to about 7 miles east of Hotchkiss.  The width of the damage patch extended from about 2 miles north of Hotchkiss to about just north of Crawford.  Numerous trees were downed, with some trees more than 2 feet in diameter where they were snapped off.  Many houses and other personal property were damaged, including the historic brick Hotchkiss Barn.  An airplane was torn from its moorings and flipped over at the Paonia Airport.  Power was out in many locations, though it was restored at most locations by the afternoon on the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough moved across the intermountain region and produced significant snowfall in many locations during one of the busiest travel times of the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 10 to 14 inches was measured.  Locally heavier amounts included 21 inches at Steamboat Springs Ski Area.  Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calving shed blown down towards the west at Road 30.5 and Road W which is one half mile north of Stratton.  Power lines were also down just north of this location.

CZ_NAME: N & NE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / N LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow coupled with very strong winds produced hazardous conditions along the Interstate 70 Corridor, east of Denver, during the early morning hours.  A peak wind gust to 53 mph was recorded observed at Limon, with northerly winds gusting to 39 mph along Interstate 70 at Bennett.  Visibilities were reduced to one quarter of a mile at times in snow and blowing snow.  Storm totals included:  3 inches, 10 miles south-southeast of Buckley AFB and 14 miles north of Arriba, with 2 inches near Hugo.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the North Central Mountains of Colorado.  Storm totals included:  19.5 inches at Columbine SNOTEL, 16.5 inches at Never Summer, 15 inches at Joe Wright, 13.5 inches at Jones Pass and Phantom Valley SNOTEL, 12 inches at the Loveland, Tower and Zirkel SNOTEL sites; 10.5 inches at Grizzly Peak and Stillwater Creek; and 10 inches near Grand Lake.  Lesser amounts of 4 to 9 inches were observed elsewhere.  In addition, a snowboarder was injured in an avalanche over Berthoud Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder was injured when he triggered an avalanche at the Nitro Chute on Berthoud Pass.  He suffered a back injury when he was transported over a cliff band.  The severity of his injuries was unknown.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy snow and strong winds produced dangerous driving conditions across the North Central Mountains.  Near blizzard conditions forced the closure of U.S. Highway 6 at Loveland Pass and U.S. Highway 40 at Berthoud Pass.   Blowing and drifting snow had also closed a 65-mile stretch of Interstate 70, between Idaho Springs and Vail Pass.  Peak wind gusts included:  68 mph atop Niwot Ridge, 62 mph at Loveland Pass, 55 mph near Blackhawk and Gunsight, and 52 mph at Berthoud Pass.||Storm totals included:  21.5 inches at the Joe Wright and Zirkel SNOTEL sites, 20 inches at Breckenridge Ski Area, 15 inches at Loveland Ski Area, 18 inches at Columbine SNOTEL, 14.5 inches at Lake Irene, Grizzly Park and Willow Park; 14 inches at Copper Mountain, 13 inches at Buffalo Creek SNOTEL and Jones Pass; with 12 inches at Arapahoe Ridge, Jackwacker Gulch and Roach SNOTEL.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

Thunderstorm outflow produced strong wind gusts over Western Mesa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow wind gusts to 58 mph were measured at a location about 5 miles north of Mack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds produced strong wind gusts in Montrose County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds of 59 mph were measured at the Montrose Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of monsoonal moisture across Southwest Colorado led to heavy rain across portions of La Plata County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1.25 inches was measured in a short period of time near Durango.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture present over the area allowed afternoon and evening thunderstorms to develop which generated very heavy rainfall over portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal fed thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall over portions of Hinsdale County and were responsible for numerous land slides along Highway 149 near Lake City. The largest slide, near Larson Creek, was estimated to be 12 foot high and contained approximately 1200 to 1500 yards of debris. Up to 60 vehicles were stranded on either side of the slide for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and damaging winds over portions of Archuleta County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts toppled a tree near Pagosa Springs, damaging the roof of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms over Southwest Colorado produced frequent cloud to ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Electricity was lost to around 234 consumers when a pole was ignited by a lightning strike that occurred on the western edge of Mancos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and damaging winds over portions of Archuleta County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates of over an inch per hour were reported in Pagosa Springs which created localized flooding. A number of reports were received of water in homes and stores, and many roads were reported as being covered by standing water.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging winds...exceeding 85 mph at times...were noted over the mountains and adjacent high valleys and plains of southeast Colorado.  The most widespread damage (including but not limited to roofs...trees...broken windows and power outages) was experienced over western portions of Colorado Springs in El Paso County. In addition, three semi-trailer trucks overturned near the Pueblo-Huerfano County line, but the drivers where not injured. A truck also overturned in Colorado Springs.

Another winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow, strong winds and very cold wind chill temperatures to the mountains, foothills and northeast plains of colorado.  The strong winds and heavy snow produced blizzard conditions over the far northeast plains, namely Logan, Sedgwick and Phillips Counties.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 30 to 45 mph and produced extensive blowing and drifting snow along with whiteout conditions.  Train spotters near Iliff, Peetz and Sterling, reported snow drifts from 3 to 5 feet in depth. ||Across the northeast plains, storm totals included:  10 inches at Iliff, 9.5 inches at Peetz, 8 inches, 5 miles south of Sedgwick; 7 inches, 4 miles south of Ovid; 6.5 inches near Akron, Amherst, 14 miles of Arriba and Brush; 6 inches, 12 miles southeast of Aurora, near Hillrose and 4 miles east-northeast of Shaw; 5 inches, 6 miles northwest of Greeley and Holyoke.||Across the Urban Corridor, storm totals included:  6 inches at Boulder and 2 miles north of Loveland; 5 inches, 2 miles east of Elizabeth and 5 miles west of Littleton;  with lesser amounts of 2 to 4 inches elsewhere.  In the mountains and foothills, storm totals generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging winds...exceeding 85 mph at times...were noted over the mountains and adjacent high valleys and plains of southeast Colorado.  The most widespread damage (including but not limited to roofs...trees...broken windows and power outages) was experienced over western portions of Colorado Springs in El Paso County. In addition, three semi-trailer trucks overturned near the Pueblo-Huerfano County line, but the drivers where not injured. A truck also overturned in Colorado Springs.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy snow and strong winds produced dangerous driving conditions across the North Central Mountains.  Near blizzard conditions forced the closure of U.S. Highway 6 at Loveland Pass and U.S. Highway 40 at Berthoud Pass.   Blowing and drifting snow had also closed a 65-mile stretch of Interstate 70, between Idaho Springs and Vail Pass.  Peak wind gusts included:  68 mph atop Niwot Ridge, 62 mph at Loveland Pass, 55 mph near Blackhawk and Gunsight, and 52 mph at Berthoud Pass.||Storm totals included:  21.5 inches at the Joe Wright and Zirkel SNOTEL sites, 20 inches at Breckenridge Ski Area, 15 inches at Loveland Ski Area, 18 inches at Columbine SNOTEL, 14.5 inches at Lake Irene, Grizzly Park and Willow Park; 14 inches at Copper Mountain, 13 inches at Buffalo Creek SNOTEL and Jones Pass; with 12 inches at Arapahoe Ridge, Jackwacker Gulch and Roach SNOTEL.  Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

Heavy rainfall, with up to two inch per hour rates, resulted in numerous reports of flooding across western Colorado including the Gunnison River which rapidly rose in the Gunnison River Gorge. The excess rainfall also caused several mud slides which temporarily closed area highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in the Gunnison River stage rapidly rising about 4 feet through the BLM's Gunnison Gorge Wilderness Area.  Numerous camp sites and trail heads were destroyed or covered in debris and boulders to the point that they were no longer usable.  The movement of boulders and silt significantly changed the characteristic of the Gunnison River in a number of places, such as the Miracle Mile rapids where rapids no longer exist.  Six river runners were trapped at the T-Dyke boater site during the event.  Numerous small fish were killed by the flash flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging winds...exceeding 85 mph at times...were noted over the mountains and adjacent high valleys and plains of southeast Colorado.  The most widespread damage (including but not limited to roofs...trees...broken windows and power outages) was experienced over western portions of Colorado Springs in El Paso County. The AWOS at the airport measured gusts to 58 mph.  There were no known eyewitnesses to this damaging wind event since it occurred shortly after midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wetting rains earlier in the month likely resulted in a fragile situation for the hillside above I-70 in De Beque Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide of boulders up to the size of refrigerators fell down onto the eastbound lanes of Interstate 70 near mile marker 46, resulting in accidents for 2 semi tractor trailers, a pickup truck, and a passenger automobile.  The boulders and damaged vehicles kept the eastbound lanes of I-70 closed for several hours until the lanes were cleared.  Boulders continued to roll down the hillside at least an hour after the intitial boulders came crashing down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm, associated with a strong west to southwesterly flow aloft, produced a 4-day period of moderate to heavy snow in the North Central Mountains.  The combination of strong wind and heavy snow forced the closure of several mountain passes due to threat of avalanches.  The Amtrack train route, which runs from Denver to California, was rerouted through Wyoming when Union Pacific closed its tracks along Interstate 70.  Numerous accidents forced the closure of I-70 at times.  Peak wind gusts over the higher mountain passes generally ranged from 45 to 60 mph.  Storm totals ranged from 16 to 32 inches in the mountains, with 5 to 10 inches in the valleys of Grand and Jackson Counties.||In the mountains, storm totals included:  32 inches, 10 miles east of Silverthorne; 29 inches at Arapahoe Basin and Loveland Ski Area; 28 inches at Breckenridge Ski Area; 23 inches, 16 miles west-southwest of Walden; 21 inches at Willow Park; 20 inches at Copper Mountain; 18 inches at Zirkel SNOTEL; 16 inches at Eldora Ski Resort, Long Draw Reservoir SNOTEL and Never Summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter event brought widespread snow to the mountains and high valleys of southern Colorado.  Winter Storm Warnings were issued for the higher elevations of the Continental Divide and the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains.  However, the lengthy period of light snow prompted Winter Weather Advisories for many other areas.  Some snow amounts were...7 inches north-northwest of Aguilar (Huerfano County)...9 to 11 inches around Beulah and Rye (Pueblo County) and Monument (El Paso County)...15 to 18 inches west of Westcliffe (Custer County) and on Monarch Pass (Chaffee County)...and 45 inches near the summit of Wolf Creek Pass.

Storm totals included: 11 inches at Willow Creek SNOTEL, 10 inches at Columbine SNOTEL and Joe Wright, 9 inches at Buffalo Park SNOTEL, Long Draw SNOTEL and Roach SNOTEL, 7 inches at Arapahoe Ridge, with 6 inches at Lake Irene.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds and tornadoes across parts of Adams, Arapahoe, Elbert, Morgan and Weld Counties.  The hail, up to half dollar size, came down so heavy along parts of Interstates 70 and 76 that snowplows had to be called out to remove it.  In Weld and Morgan Counties, very heavy rain and hail accompanied thunderstorm winds up to 75 mph.  The heavy rain caused some street flooding in parts of Greeley.  Approximately 1300 residents lost power briefly in the towns of Johnston and Milliken.  Two brief tornados were observed in Adams and Elbert Counties but no damage was reported.  Several minor accidents were reported with snowpacked and slick road conditions along with very low visibilities.

Several inches of fresh snow fell on top of an unstable snow-pack leading to a skier triggered avalanche which carried two skiers and resulted in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven back-country skiers ventured out on a hut trip near the Ridgeway Hut in the northern San Juan Mountains when two of the skiers were caught in an avalanche. Nearby observations indicated anywhere from 10 to 16 inches of fresh snow had fallen in the 6 days leading up to the event. The skiers were attempting their second run of the day when they decided to split into two groups. One of the skiers in the second group triggered the slide which then carried two of the skiers about 400 feet downhill. One skier was completely buried under 1 to 2 feet of snow, while the other skier was thrown into a large tree. Other party members carrying rescue equipment immediately began searching for the caught skiers. Both skiers were dug out of the snow and brought to the hospital in Montrose by rescue helicopter. The buried skier did not survive, while the other skier suffered multiple traumatic injuries, including a dislocated hip.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall from a strong winter storm was deposited onto a weak, preexisting snow-pack, which lead to a deadly skier triggered avalanche just above the Lindley Hut near Ashcroft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the week leading up to the date of the accident, Aspen Mountain, which is the closest observing site, reported up to 35 inches of new snowfall. Weak layers underneath the fresh snow created a very unstable snowpack, which lead to the deadly avalanche. A group of 6 people were staying at the Lindley Hut near Ashcroft, and on the day of the accident, 3 people in the group ventured out to ski some of the lower slopes near the hut. The skiers were skinning up a slope about a quarter mile east of the hut, when the first skier triggered a slide that carried him 150 feet downhill and buried him underneath 3 feet of snow in a dense stand of trees. The two other skiers did not witness the initial trigger of the slide, but began to search for the victim after seeing what happened. They contacted Mountain Rescue Aspen who were flown into the site the next morning and eventually found the victim near the toe of the avalanche debris.

A potent spring storm brought heavy, wet snow to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills and widespread rainfall to the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  In the Front Range Foothills and North-Central Mountains, mainly east of the Continental Divide, storm totals generally ranged from 15 to 30 inches.  Storm totals over the Palmer Ridge ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  Across the Northeast Plains, rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.5 to 2.5 inches over the 2-day period.  One tornado reportedly touched down, but did no damage.||The more impressive storm totals included:  29.5 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 27 inches, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 23 inches at Willow Creek, 22.5 inches, 13 miles northwest of Golden 21 inches at Never Summer, 18 inches at Buckhorn Mountain, 14 miles north-northwest of Glendevey, and Long Draw Reservoir SNOTEL; 17 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 16.5 inches, 3 miles west of Jamestown; with 16 inches at Arapahoe Ridge and Elliot Ridge SNOTEL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly but did no damage.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to the mountains near Aspen throughout the week leading up to the accident. This added a significant load to the underlying weak snow surface and resulted in an avalanche just outside of Aspen Mountain Ski Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of February 26th, a group of four back-country skiers exited the Aspen Mountain Ski Area boundaries and entered into Ophir Gulch. Two of the skiers in the group were on the steeper part of the terrain when an avalanche was triggered by one of them. The other skier was below digging a pit when the avalanche began and was carried a short distance by the slide before he was able to grab hold of a tree and avoid being swept further downhill. The other skier, who initially triggered the slide, was carried 500 vertical feet and was buried on the uphill side of a tree. The buried skier was quickly found and attended to by his partners, but the rescue required the assistance of nine Aspen Mountain Ski Patrol members. The victim who sustained serious chest and neck injuries was brought to an ambulance by 8:40 pm that evening.

A potent spring storm brought heavy, wet snow to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills and widespread rainfall to the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  In the Front Range Foothills and North-Central Mountains, mainly east of the Continental Divide, storm totals generally ranged from 15 to 30 inches.  Storm totals over the Palmer Ridge ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  Across the Northeast Plains, rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.5 to 2.5 inches over the 2-day period.  One tornado reportedly touched down, but did no damage.||The more impressive storm totals included:  29.5 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 27 inches, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 23 inches at Willow Creek, 22.5 inches, 13 miles northwest of Golden 21 inches at Never Summer, 18 inches at Buckhorn Mountain, 14 miles north-northwest of Glendevey, and Long Draw Reservoir SNOTEL; 17 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 16.5 inches, 3 miles west of Jamestown; with 16 inches at Arapahoe Ridge and Elliot Ridge SNOTEL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  27 inches, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 18 inches at Buckhorn Mountain and 14 miles north-northwest of Glendevey,  16.5 inches, 3 miles west of Jamestown; 14 inches, 7 miles southwest of Boulder; with 11 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of back-country skiers triggered an avalanche on Mt. Yeckel above the town of Lenado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of four back-country skiers were skinning up Mt. Yeckel to attempt their second run for the day, when the lead skier triggered an avalanche. The avalanche carried the first skier 100 feet downhill, where he then hit a tree. The second of the four skiers was also carried downhill but remained on top of the snow surface. The remaining two skiers were able to stay out of the avalanche path. The uninjured skiers contacted Mountain Rescue Aspen, who attended to the injured skier around 3:00 am the next morning. He was flown out by helicopter about 3 hours later and was diagnosed with a broken lower leg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of February saw improving drought conditions across western Colorado. Portions of southwest Colorado improved from abnormally dry to near normal conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions in the western portion improved to near normal by the end of the month, while normal conditions persisted in the eastern area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds and tornadoes across parts of Adams, Arapahoe, Elbert, Morgan and Weld Counties.  The hail, up to half dollar size, came down so heavy along parts of Interstates 70 and 76 that snowplows had to be called out to remove it.  In Weld and Morgan Counties, very heavy rain and hail accompanied thunderstorm winds up to 75 mph.  The heavy rain caused some street flooding in parts of Greeley.  Approximately 1300 residents lost power briefly in the towns of Johnston and Milliken.  Two brief tornados were observed in Adams and Elbert Counties but no damage was reported.  Several minor accidents were reported with snowpacked and slick road conditions along with very low visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were buried and perished when up to 2 1/2 feet of snow slid off a cabin roof 17 miles west of Creede.  This was the first instance of a fatal roof avalanche since 1996 in Vail.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm brought heavy, wet snow to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills and widespread rainfall to the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  In the Front Range Foothills and North-Central Mountains, mainly east of the Continental Divide, storm totals generally ranged from 15 to 30 inches.  Storm totals over the Palmer Ridge ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  Across the Northeast Plains, rainfall totals generally ranged from 1.5 to 2.5 inches over the 2-day period.  One tornado reportedly touched down, but did no damage.||The more impressive storm totals included:  29.5 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 27 inches, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 23 inches at Willow Creek, 22.5 inches, 13 miles northwest of Golden 21 inches at Never Summer, 18 inches at Buckhorn Mountain, 14 miles north-northwest of Glendevey, and Long Draw Reservoir SNOTEL; 17 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 16.5 inches, 3 miles west of Jamestown; with 16 inches at Arapahoe Ridge and Elliot Ridge SNOTEL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  29.5 inches, 3 miles southeast of Pinecliffe, 22.5 inches, 13 miles northwest of Golden; 17 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs, with 14.5 inches at Genesee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into the Great Basin Region pulling a strong cold front with it. The front stalled over northeast Utah, where it produced heavy snowfall over the eastern Utah mountains. The front then pushed through western Colorado spreading heavy snow over the Colorado mountains and higher valleys across northwest Colorado. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall produced accumulations of 6 to 10 inches across mainly northern and eastern portions of the area. Wind gusts up to 50 mph generated extensive blowing and drifting snow.  The heavy, wet snow weighed down tree limbs and power lines, which resulted in several power outages across the Yampa Valley affecting areas from Craig to Hayden to Steamboat Springs. Over 500 residents were without power This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into the Great Basin Region pulling a strong cold front with it. The front stalled over northeast Utah, where it produced heavy snowfall over the eastern Utah mountains. The front then pushed through western Colorado spreading heavy snow over the Colorado mountains and higher valleys across northwest Colorado. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 13 inches. Locally higher amounts were reported, including 16 inches in Clark. Winds gusted up to 35 mph producing areas of blowing snow.  The heavy, wet snow weighed down tree limbs and power lines, which resulted in several power outages across the Yampa Valley affecting areas from Craig to Hayden to Steamboat Springs. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early January snowfall overloaded an underlying weak snow surface, causing a deadly avalanche to occur just outside of Vail Mountains Ski Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder was assumed to have exited Vail Ski Area in Blue Sky Basin, where he proceeded toward the Miller Cliffs on Battle Mountain. While attempting to snowboard outside of ski area boundaries, he triggered 2 small avalanches that came together and carried him roughly 100 feet downhill. He was carried into a stand of trees where he was partially buried head-first and suffocated under 3 feet of snow. The snowboarder was alone and was not recovered until nearly a week later on January 14. Therefore, the time of this accident is estimated.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Vail Ski Area reported over 2 feet of snow in a seven day period followed by strong west to northwest winds that transported snow and built fresh, unstable slabs. These unstable snow pack conditions resulted in an avalanche release just outside of Vail Ski Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four experienced backcountry skiers headed out-of-bounds and toward Old Man's Bowl just outside of the East Vail area. One of the skiers attempted to ski down the bowl and made a ski cut below the ridgeline heading towards the tree band which separates two portions of East Marvin's. He triggered a small soft slab avalanche  which ran about 200 vertical feet onto a bench. The skier then skied onto and below the bench and triggered a second soft slab avalanche that was about 100 feet wide and ran 400 vertical feet. The skier attempted to ski out to the side and out of the way of the debris flow. While looking over his shoulder he lost his balance, fell, and was caught in the debris and washed down the slope. The skier was buried with his head just below the snow surface.  The other skiers in the group were able to dig him out of the snow. The buried skier sustained a broken arm and several cuts and bruises to his head and limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder was caught in an avalanche near Silverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two snowboarders rode down the northwest face of a mountain about four miles northeast of Silverton. The first rider ski cut the top of the open face above the trees, and then rode down 100 vertical feet. The second snowboarder began riding down the slope and triggered an avalanched that started with an upper face fracture of 3 feet deep and 50 feet wide. The first snowboarder waiting below could not escape the slide path and pinned himself on the uphill side of a tree. The slide moved through his location and buried him to mid thigh. The two snowmobilers then proceeded down the avalanche path, which was between 400 and 500 vertical feet. There were no injuries.  The exact time of the avalanche is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler was caught in an avalanche west of Crested Butte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler with a sled got stuck in flats below a mountain slope near Irwin Lodge. A second snowmobiler drove just above the stuck snowmobiler and triggered three separate avalanches. One of the avalanches came down onto the stuck snowmobiler. His sled rolled on top of him and he was partially buried with his head and hand just above the snow.  The path of that avalanche was 400 vertical feet and was 80 feet wide.  No significant injuries or damage were reported.  The time of the avalanche is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms brought anywhere from 1 to over 3 feet of snow to the Durango area, which caused an avalanche to release in the San Juan mountains that blasted into a vacant home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the northwest side of Animas Mountain within the Durango city limits, an avalanche roared into a house. The homeowners were about to move into the 15 year old home within the following 2 weeks. Snow blasted through an exterior wall and blew loose drywall onto an interior wall. One bedroom was completely filled with snow, and the home suffered severe structural damage. The avalanche ran about 600 vertical feet with a 150 foot wide path. The damaged home was discovered Sunday, January 24, but the actual time of the avalanche is unknown.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring snowstorm swept across North Central and Northeast Colorado.  In and near the Front Range Foothills, storm totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Elsewhere, storm totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches.  The heavy, wet snow clung to power lines and tree limbs, causing 36,500 outages to homes and businesses throughout the Denver metro area.  Power outages were also reported in Douglas and Elbert Counties.  Approximately 5,000 travelers were stranded at Denver International Airport due to numerous cancellations and delays.  Initally, the snow fell at a rate to 2 to 4 inches per hour between Boulder and Denver, bringing the evening rush hour to a complete halt.  Countless motorists were stuck on U.S. Highway 36 for several hours.  Other road closures included eastbound Interstate 70, between Silverthorne and the Eisenhower Tunnel; and Interstate 70, from Idaho Springs to Genesee; Interstate 25 in both directions from Lincoln Avenue to Greenland, about 15 miles south of Castle Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included: 12 inches, 3 miles west of Fort Collins; 10.5 inches, 2 miles north of Loveland; 8 inches in Loveland; 6.5 inches, 1 mile southeast of Fort Collins; 6 inches at Colorado State University in Fort Collins and Wellington.

Dead tree limbs were blown down and a sign was damaged at an auto repair shop in Yuma.

A powerful spring snowstorm swept across North Central and Northeast Colorado.  In and near the Front Range Foothills, storm totals ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Elsewhere, storm totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches.  The heavy, wet snow clung to power lines and tree limbs, causing 36,500 outages to homes and businesses throughout the Denver metro area.  Power outages were also reported in Douglas and Elbert Counties.  Approximately 5,000 travelers were stranded at Denver International Airport due to numerous cancellations and delays.  Initally, the snow fell at a rate to 2 to 4 inches per hour between Boulder and Denver, bringing the evening rush hour to a complete halt.  Countless motorists were stuck on U.S. Highway 36 for several hours.  Other road closures included eastbound Interstate 70, between Silverthorne and the Eisenhower Tunnel; and Interstate 70, from Idaho Springs to Genesee; Interstate 25 in both directions from Lincoln Avenue to Greenland, about 15 miles south of Castle Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  10 inches in Fredrick and 10 miles east-southeast of Longmont; 9 inches, 3.5 miles east-southeast of Greeley; 8 inches near Milliken; 7.5 inches, 4 miles northwest of Greeley; and 6 inches, 5 miles west-southwest of Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across northeast Colorado into southwest Nebraska during the evening and moved southeast.  Several reports of damaging winds were received resulting in utility pole, tree and farm damage in the Burlington Colorado and Goodland Kansas areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven power poles were blown down on Road BB about 1.5 miles east of highway 385.  A parked farm trailer was overturned near highway 385 approximately 3/4 mile north of road BB.  A cherry tree was split in half and irrigation sprinklers were damaged. Numerous tree branches were down in and just east of Burlington.  Time estimated from Doppler radar.  This event appeared to result from an intense downburst with a path length of approximately 7 miles, with damaging laying down to the south and southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across Cheyenne County overnight resulting in an isolated report of marginally severe hail.

A line of thunderstorms developed in eastern Colorado during the late afternoon hours and eventually intensified, containing several embedded supercells. One supercell produced a tornado in extreme southern Kit Carson county which lasted 4 minutes. Later, a severe wind gust was reported by the ASOS station just south of Burlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed from a well-defined wall cloud and lasted approximately 4 minutes.  The tornado remained over rural, open country with no known damage.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A downed power line started a wildfire in southwest Colorado near Norwood, which was propelled by very strong winds and dry conditions. This fire began in May and persisted for several days through the beginning of June. Please see the May 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2:30 pm on the afternoon of Saturday, May 22, strong winds downed a power line which sparked a wildfire near the border of San Miguel and Montrose Counties along Good Enough Road. The fire spread rapidly with dry conditions and very strong southwest winds in place. Highway 145 was closed for a period of time on May 5th due to the fire.  Portions of the San Miguel River were also closed due to the wildfire hazard. The fire continued for several days and eventually burned through roughly 2,500 acres of the Uncompahgre National Forest and nearly 430 acres of BLM land. There were no injuries or personal property damage reported, but the estimated cost of fighting the fire was $1,500,000. This fire began in May, so please see the May 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of intense thunderstorms moved southeast across Yuma county into northwest Kansas during the evening.  One tornado developed in Yuma county, and a large swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced reports of a swath of hail from 1 west of Yuma to 3 east of Yuma with multiple reports of 2.75 inch diameter hail.  In addition, it is estimated that over 26,000 acres of crops were damaged or destroyed.  Many windows were broken in homes and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but intense cluster of storms moved southeast across Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties producing golf ball to baseball sized hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large pickup towing a long, flatbed trailer was blown over on Interstate 70 blocking the west bound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind event the previous day lead to a few natural avalanches in the Uncompahgre Gorge through the overnight hours of April 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong wind event the previous day resulted in the release of at least 3 natural avalanches that ran across Highway 550 in the Uncompahgre Gorge area, closing the highway for a period of time. The avalanche deposits on the highway ranged from 1 to 5 feet deep and from 20 to 80 foot long sections of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increase in moisture brought a significant precipitation event to areas of southern Colorado where snow melt was already in progress.  This resulted in flooding and out of bank flows along portions of the Navajo River and Stollsteimer Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stollsteimer Creek ran out of its banks in some places within the Aspen Springs subdivision west of Pagosa Springs, which inundated some foot bridges and flooded a trailer that was flooded.  A portion of County Road 359 was also washed out due to flowing water exceeding the capacity of a 4 foot diameter culvert underneath the road. Additionally, the Navajo River also flowed out of its banks near the confluence of the Navajo and San Juan Rivers along County Road 551.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of 8 back-country skiers were participating in an advanced back-country skiing course when one of the skiers triggered an avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 15 to 17 inches were reported on the northern side of the San Juan mountains just prior to the date of this event, which contributed to unstable snow conditions. A group of 8 back-country skiers headed into the mountains northwest of Silverton for an advanced back-country skiing course. After ascending the slope, the first skier in the group skied down and stopped in a safe patch of trees to wait for the others. The next 5 skiers descended one at a time about 1,000 feet downhill and stopped to wait for the others. The seventh skier descended and fell into a small concavity which released the slide that propagated out in both directions. Only the first and seventh skier were caught and carried in the avalanche, but were both able to recover with no injuries. None of the other skiers were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow in the San Juan Mountains combined with weak underlying snow surfaces and the warmest temperatures of the season so far to produce a fatal avalanche near Baldy Peak, southeast of the town of Ridgeway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms in March brought several feet of snow to the San Juan Mountains, including the storm leading up to this event which deposited 21 inches of new snowfall at the nearest observation site. The date of this event was the warmest day of the season to date with strong southerly winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to around 60 mph. All of these factors combined to produce what was believed to be a natural avalanche near Baldy Peak. A very experienced back-country traveler and certified alpine mountain guide with professional avalanche training headed out to attempt a remote ice climb on the northeast side of Baldy Peak when she was caught in the avalanche. Because she was traveling alone, many of the details of this event are unknown or estimated. The following day a search and rescue team found the avalanche site along with some of the climber's equipment and her dog, but were unsuccessful in finding the climber. They returned the following day with a larger team and eventually found the body of the missing climber buried under 5 to 6 feet of snow and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms pounded parts the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains with very large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes.  The hail, ranging from golfball to baseball size, hit northeast Denver, Brighton and Commerce City the hardest.  The storms then continued to spread to the north and east; impacting Hudson, Prospect Valley, Byers and Deer Trail.   The strong thunderstorm wind and hail stripped bark and branches from trees.  Numerous accidents were reported as the hail accumulated up to a foot in depth on roadways.  Homes and vehicles in the area were pummeled by hail.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Associated estimated to damage to be around $70 million; based off approximately 13,600 automobile and homeowner claims.  A child in Commerce City was struck in the leg by lightning while watching televison.  A bison and the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge was struck and killed by blast of lightning.  Two tornadoes also touched down in open country; one near Denver International Airport and the other near Keenesburg.  ||The heavy rain and hail caused flash flooding to occur around the towns of Hudson and Keenseburg; forcing the closure of State Highway 52.  Extensive crop damage was reported across southern Weld County.  Approximately 1500 acres of sugar beets were damaged in the Prospect Valley and Windsor areas.  About 360 acres of sweet corn, cabbage and onions in the Hudson area were inundated with heavy rain and a torrent of hail.  In Windsor, a barn was demolished by intense thunderstorm winds.   The wind ripped the 40-ft by 40-ft structure of a garage and exterior corrugated roof and walls about 75 yards from its original foundation. Also, two cow shelters and 150 calf shelters were blown over.  Near Bellvue, several structures were knocked down and a 15-ft silo was blown across a field by damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail across Otero, Bent, and Kiowa Counties. A strong winter storm system moved into the Great Basin region pulling a strong cold front with it. The front pushed through western Colorado where it spread heavy snow over the Colorado mountains and higher valleys of northwest Colorado. This event continued into April, so please see the April 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall produced accumulations ranging from 9 to 20 inches. Wind gusts ranging from 40 to 60 mph generated extensive blowing and drifting snow. This event continued into April, so please see the April 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms pounded parts the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains with very large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes.  The hail, ranging from golfball to baseball size, hit northeast Denver, Brighton and Commerce City the hardest.  The storms then continued to spread to the north and east; impacting Hudson, Prospect Valley, Byers and Deer Trail.   The strong thunderstorm wind and hail stripped bark and branches from trees.  Numerous accidents were reported as the hail accumulated up to a foot in depth on roadways.  Homes and vehicles in the area were pummeled by hail.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Associated estimated to damage to be around $70 million; based off approximately 13,600 automobile and homeowner claims.  A child in Commerce City was struck in the leg by lightning while watching televison.  A bison and the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge was struck and killed by blast of lightning.  Two tornadoes also touched down in open country; one near Denver International Airport and the other near Keenesburg.  ||The heavy rain and hail caused flash flooding to occur around the towns of Hudson and Keenseburg; forcing the closure of State Highway 52.  Extensive crop damage was reported across southern Weld County.  Approximately 1500 acres of sugar beets were damaged in the Prospect Valley and Windsor areas.  About 360 acres of sweet corn, cabbage and onions in the Hudson area were inundated with heavy rain and a torrent of hail.  In Windsor, a barn was demolished by intense thunderstorm winds.   The wind ripped the 40-ft by 40-ft structure of a garage and exterior corrugated roof and walls about 75 yards from its original foundation. Also, two cow shelters and 150 calf shelters were blown over.  Near Bellvue, several structures were knocked down and a 15-ft silo was blown across a field by damaging thunderstorm winds.
A strong thunderstorm produced hail up to the size of pennies in northern El Paso County.

A low pressure storm system moved from western Utah into south-central New Mexico spreading rain and snow across much of western Colorado. The mountains and some lower elevation areas of west central and southwest Colorado received significant snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches occurred. Wind gusts to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped out of British Columbia and into the Central Rockies producing heavy snow over many western Colorado mountain locations, as well as significant snowfall in some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. The heaviest accumulations favored the Uncompahgre Gorge on the northern side of the San Juan mountains, where nearly 30 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches.  Wind gusts to 25 mph produced areas of drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped out of British Columbia and into the Central Rockies producing heavy snow over many western Colorado mountain locations, as well as significant snowfall in some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. The heaviest accumulations favored the Uncompahgre Gorge on the northern side of the San Juan mountains, where nearly 30 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches occurred above the 7,000 foot level. Wind gusts to 30 mph produced some areas of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped out of British Columbia and into the Central Rockies producing heavy snow over many western Colorado mountain locations, as well as significant snowfall in some of the lower valleys of western Colorado. The heaviest accumulations favored the Uncompahgre Gorge on the northern side of the San Juan mountains, where nearly 30 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow on the backside of the low pressure system produced heavy snowfall, especially in the Uncompahgre Gorge area where accumulations of up to 30 inches were reported in the town of Ouray. Snowfall amounts elsewhere in the zone ranged from 8 to 18 inches. Wind gusts up to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow. The adverse weather conditions, along with several natural avalanches, prompted the closure of Red Mountain Pass along Highway 550. Travel restrictions were also put into effect along Highway 145.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked along the Utah-Colorado border. Snowfall from this storm favored the northern and central mountains of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 12 inches. Wind gusts to 30 mph generated areas of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season storm system spread heavy snowfall over the Colorado mountains as well as some of the higher valleys. A few of the lower valleys in western Colorado also received significant late season snowfall accumulations. Gusty winds up to 35 mph produced some areas of blowing and drifting snow. This event continues into May, so please see the May 2010 Storm Data Publication for further details.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of very heavy rain and large hail produced flash flooding along State Highway 52 near Hudson and Keenseburg.  Up to 18 inches of water flowed across sections of the highway, damaging the roadway.  The most severe damage was reported at the intersection of State Highway 52 and County Road 49.  Extensive crop damage was also reported in the area.  The highway remained closed until the floodwaters subsided.

A small but intense cluster of storms moved southeast across Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties producing golf ball to baseball sized hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reports later described many vehicles on Hwy 24 and Interstate 70 suffering broken windshields and body damage. Hail also damaged crops in the area and the storm resulted in heavy runoff for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped heavy rainfall, small hail and produced damaging winds over southeast Yuma county during the evening.  Flash flooding near Bonny Reservoir resulted in road closures overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many county roads were closed overnight in the vicinity of Bonny Reservoir and the small town of Hale.  One county road had a culvert washed out.  A resident 1 mile north and 1 mile west of Bonny Lake reported at least 10 inches of rain, stating his gauge holds ten inches and it filled up and overflowed.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The southern and central mountains were favored, as well as the southern Colorado and southeast Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 6 to 14 inches occurred. Wind gusts up to 25 mph produced localized blowing snow.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The southern and central mountains were favored, as well as the southern Colorado and southeast Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 8 to 17 inches occurred. Locally higher amounts included 19 inches about 4 miles northwest of Colona and 23 inches near Crawford. Wind gusts of 25 mph produced localized blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred across about half of the area with amounts ranging from 6 to 10 inches. Meanwhile, areas extending from Fruita eastward into Palisade generally saw 2 inches or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough extending from an upper low in southern Canada brushed the region with significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 12 inches occurred across the area.  Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across Yuma county during the evening, producing locally heavy rain and an isolated hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain also occurred with water ponding at the intersection of Hwy 385 and Hwy 34.

CZ_NAME: CANON CITY VICINITY / EASTERN FREMONT COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire which consumed around 700 acres began near railroad tracks and spread to the top of 8 Mile Hill.  It prompted evacuations and burned several structures belonging to the Fremont County coroner, including her residence.  Many tourist attractions, including the Royal Gorge Bridge, were evacuated.  Slightly fewer than 200 people fought the wildfire.  Containment continued into July.
Strong thunderstorms produced hail up to nickel size.
Strong thunderstorms produced hail up to nickel size.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size in Adams County.  In Arapahoe County, hail up to penny size was observed.  The hail accumulated to 5 inches in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated up to 5 inches in depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season storm system spread heavy snowfall over the Colorado mountains as well as some of the higher valleys. A few of the lower valleys in western Colorado also received significant late season snowfall accumulations. This event continued into May, so please see the May 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 12 inches occurred across most of the area with some locally higher amounts, including 17 inches in Ouray. Wind gusts ranged from 30 to 60 mph which generated some areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 10 inches occurred across most of the area. Wind gusts ranged from 30 to 60 mph which generated some areas of blowing and drifting snow.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions throughout the month of March remained unchanged over southwest portions of the state, where conditions remained near normal. The northwest portion of the state continued to see abnormally dry conditions, with a portion of Routt county worsening to moderate drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions in the southern portion of the area at the beginning to the month improved by the end of the month to a situation with no drought.  The northern portion of the area did not experience any drought conditions throughout the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of precipitation and a recent freeze-thaw cycle loosened rocks and boulders in Glenwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive rockslide occurred in Glenwood Canyon about two miles east of Glenwood springs and struck a section of Interstate 70 with boulders the size of trucks.  Some of the boulders punched through the westbound lanes producing holes in the road up to 20 feet in length and 10 feet wide.  Additionally, large boulders the size of cars blocked the eastbound lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter size hail near Centenniel.
A snowmobiler triggered an avalanche in the Flat Tops area of the White River National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One male snowmobiler was ascending a slope near Cliff Lake Overlook in the Flat Tops Wilderness Area when he triggered an avalanche. The rider fell off of his machine near the top of the slope, which triggered the slide. He and his machine were carried about 100 feet downhill, but he was able to ride the avalanche in a sitting position and was only partially buried.  The victim did not sustain any serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two cross country skiers were caught in an avalanche on the Grand Mesa and one of them sustained serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 1:00 pm on March 17th, two cross country skiers attempted to ski the Thunderbird area on the west side of the Grand Mesa. The skiers were passing through a clearing when a wall of snow above them collapsed. They were both carried an estimated 300 to 800 feet down slope. One of the skiers was dragged into several trees and seriously injured. The second skier was able to contact the Sheriff's Department, who dispatched a search and rescue team that eventually airlifted them to St. Mary's Hospital where the injured skier was put into the intensive care unit. The injured skier suffered two broken legs from the accident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down but did no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination late spring snowmelt and a series of thunderstorms producing heavy rain caused flooding across a section of the Cache La Poudre River.  Most of the flooding occcured along the river between Windsor and Greeley.  In Greeley, flooding was reported  at 93rd Avenue, between Poudre River Drive and O Street; between Poudre River Trail and O Street; and between the Fern Avenue Bridge and the County Road 53 Bridge. The floodwaters forced the closure of State Highway 52, between County Roads 65 and 73.  Flooding was also reported on County Roads 47, 49, 51 and 53; south of the highway.  In addition, flooding of small creeks and drainage ditches produced extensive field flooding around Prospect Valley.

An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The southern and central mountains were favored, as well as the southern Colorado and southeast Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 24 to 39 inches occurred. Wind gusts up to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

CZ_NAME: TRINIDAD VICINITY / LOWER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN & WESTERN LAS ANIMAS COUNTY BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gradient winds...gusting up to 75 mph caused damage...especially over portions of El Paso and Pueblo Counties.  Several large trees were uprooted in the Monte Vista area in Rio Grande County.  A modular building was flipped on its top by the wind in Pueblo West. A few trees were also uprooted in the City of Pueblo.  Winds caused power lines to come down in Manitou Springs, which sparked several small grass fires near the Pikes Peak Cog Railway.  Three hangers at Peterson Air Force Base were evacuated, and some minor roof damage occurred.  Some trees were uprooted in the greater Colorado Springs area.  On Fort Carson, eight soldiers were injured when a tent they were setting up blew over.  One soldier suffered a broken leg.

Severe thunderstorms pounded parts the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains with very large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes.  The hail, ranging from golfball to baseball size, hit northeast Denver, Brighton and Commerce City the hardest.  The storms then continued to spread to the north and east; impacting Hudson, Prospect Valley, Byers and Deer Trail.   The strong thunderstorm wind and hail stripped bark and branches from trees.  Numerous accidents were reported as the hail accumulated up to a foot in depth on roadways.  Homes and vehicles in the area were pummeled by hail.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Associated estimated to damage to be around $70 million; based off approximately 13,600 automobile and homeowner claims.  A child in Commerce City was struck in the leg by lightning while watching televison.  A bison and the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge was struck and killed by blast of lightning.  Two tornadoes also touched down in open country; one near Denver International Airport and the other near Keenesburg.  ||The heavy rain and hail caused flash flooding to occur around the towns of Hudson and Keenseburg; forcing the closure of State Highway 52.  Extensive crop damage was reported across southern Weld County.  Approximately 1500 acres of sugar beets were damaged in the Prospect Valley and Windsor areas.  About 360 acres of sweet corn, cabbage and onions in the Hudson area were inundated with heavy rain and a torrent of hail.  In Windsor, a barn was demolished by intense thunderstorm winds.   The wind ripped the 40-ft by 40-ft structure of a garage and exterior corrugated roof and walls about 75 yards from its original foundation. Also, two cow shelters and 150 calf shelters were blown over.  Near Bellvue, several structures were knocked down and a 15-ft silo was blown across a field by damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small structures were knocked down and a silo blown across a field.

A large complex of thunderstorms swept across Northeast Colorado producing very large damaging hail, intense thunderstorm winds as well as a couple of weak tornadoes.  Large hail, from 1 to 3 inches in diameter, caused extensive damage to cropland, homes and vehicles.  In southeast Weld County, 50,000 acres of cropland was pummelled.  The combination of heavy rain and hail produced flash flooding over parts of Phillips and Weld Counties.  Electrical outages were also reported in Weld County.  Straight-line winds snapped 6 utility poles near Amherst in Phillips County.  Two small tornado also touched down but no damage was reported.

Straight-line winds snapped 6 utility poles near Amherst in Phillips County.  Two small tornado also touched down but no damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and torrential hail produced flash flooding over southeast Weld County.  Fifty thousand acres of cropland was heavily damaged.  Ham radio spotters reported water running across State Highway 52, east of Prospect Valley, forcing its closure.  In addition, State Highways 14 and 392 near Briggdale were also inundated with water.  Several county roads were either flooded or completely washed out.

Severe thunderstorms pounded parts the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains with very large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes.  The hail, ranging from golfball to baseball size, hit northeast Denver, Brighton and Commerce City the hardest.  The storms then continued to spread to the north and east; impacting Hudson, Prospect Valley, Byers and Deer Trail.   The strong thunderstorm wind and hail stripped bark and branches from trees.  Numerous accidents were reported as the hail accumulated up to a foot in depth on roadways.  Homes and vehicles in the area were pummeled by hail.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Associated estimated to damage to be around $70 million; based off approximately 13,600 automobile and homeowner claims.  A child in Commerce City was struck in the leg by lightning while watching televison.  A bison and the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge was struck and killed by blast of lightning.  Two tornadoes also touched down in open country; one near Denver International Airport and the other near Keenesburg.  ||The heavy rain and hail caused flash flooding to occur around the towns of Hudson and Keenseburg; forcing the closure of State Highway 52.  Extensive crop damage was reported across southern Weld County.  Approximately 1500 acres of sugar beets were damaged in the Prospect Valley and Windsor areas.  About 360 acres of sweet corn, cabbage and onions in the Hudson area were inundated with heavy rain and a torrent of hail.  In Windsor, a barn was demolished by intense thunderstorm winds.   The wind ripped the 40-ft by 40-ft structure of a garage and exterior corrugated roof and walls about 75 yards from its original foundation. Also, two cow shelters and 150 calf shelters were blown over.  Near Bellvue, several structures were knocked down and a 15-ft silo was blown across a field by damaging thunderstorm winds.

Gradient winds...gusting up to 75 mph caused damage...especially over portions of El Paso and Pueblo Counties.  Several large trees were uprooted in the Monte Vista area in Rio Grande County.  A modular building was flipped on its top by the wind in Pueblo West. A few trees were also uprooted in the City of Pueblo.  Winds caused power lines to come down in Manitou Springs, which sparked several small grass fires near the Pikes Peak Cog Railway.  Three hangers at Peterson Air Force Base were evacuated, and some minor roof damage occurred.  Some trees were uprooted in the greater Colorado Springs area.  On Fort Carson, eight soldiers were injured when a tent they were setting up blew over.  One soldier suffered a broken leg.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail over portions of Kiowa and Baca Counties.  Numerous non-mesocyclone tornadoes (landspouts) occured in eastern Kiowa County, yielding no significant damage.  Two tornadoes in east central Baca County damaged a mobile home and outbuildings, and destroyed fencing and cottonwood trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout occurred over open country and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced flash flooding on the east side of Colorado Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 1 1/2 feet of water covered roadways near the Chapel Hills Mall and on Powers Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail, ranging in size from quarters to baseballs.  The hail reportedly piled up to 8 inches deep in Elbert County where the largest hail was observed.  Extensive damage was reported to vehicles near Interstate 70 and State Highway 86.  Intense thunderstorm winds damaged trees and building in Genoa.  Heavy rain produced flash flooding in parts of Elbert and Larimer Counties.  One tornado touched down near Karval.  It reportedly uprooted several trees and destroyed a garage.

A large complex of thunderstorms swept across Northeast Colorado producing very large damaging hail, intense thunderstorm winds as well as a couple of weak tornadoes.  Large hail, from 1 to 3 inches in diameter, caused extensive damage to cropland, homes and vehicles.  In southeast Weld County, 50,000 acres of cropland was pummelled.  The combination of heavy rain and hail produced flash flooding over parts of Phillips and Weld Counties.  Electrical outages were also reported in Weld County.  Straight-line winds snapped 6 utility poles near Amherst in Phillips County.  Two small tornado also touched down but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed out sections of 20 country roads in Phillips County.

Severe thunderstorms pounded parts the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains with very large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes.  The hail, ranging from golfball to baseball size, hit northeast Denver, Brighton and Commerce City the hardest.  The storms then continued to spread to the north and east; impacting Hudson, Prospect Valley, Byers and Deer Trail.   The strong thunderstorm wind and hail stripped bark and branches from trees.  Numerous accidents were reported as the hail accumulated up to a foot in depth on roadways.  Homes and vehicles in the area were pummeled by hail.   The Rocky Mountain Insurance Information Associated estimated to damage to be around $70 million; based off approximately 13,600 automobile and homeowner claims.  A child in Commerce City was struck in the leg by lightning while watching televison.  A bison and the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge was struck and killed by blast of lightning.  Two tornadoes also touched down in open country; one near Denver International Airport and the other near Keenesburg.  ||The heavy rain and hail caused flash flooding to occur around the towns of Hudson and Keenseburg; forcing the closure of State Highway 52.  Extensive crop damage was reported across southern Weld County.  Approximately 1500 acres of sugar beets were damaged in the Prospect Valley and Windsor areas.  About 360 acres of sweet corn, cabbage and onions in the Hudson area were inundated with heavy rain and a torrent of hail.  In Windsor, a barn was demolished by intense thunderstorm winds.   The wind ripped the 40-ft by 40-ft structure of a garage and exterior corrugated roof and walls about 75 yards from its original foundation. Also, two cow shelters and 150 calf shelters were blown over.  Near Bellvue, several structures were knocked down and a 15-ft silo was blown across a field by damaging thunderstorm winds.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail over portions of Kiowa and Baca Counties.  Numerous non-mesocyclone tornadoes (landspouts) occured in eastern Kiowa County, yielding no significant damage.  Two tornadoes in east central Baca County damaged a mobile home and outbuildings, and destroyed fencing and cottonwood trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved northwest and damaged a mobile home and some outbuildings, then moved over open fields.

Intense thunderstorm winds damaged trees and building in Genoa.  Heavy rain produced flash flooding in parts of Elbert and Larimer Counties.  One tornado touched down near Karval.  It reportedly uprooted several trees and destroyed a garage.

Gradient winds...gusting up to 75 mph caused damage...especially over portions of El Paso and Pueblo Counties.  Several large trees were uprooted in the Monte Vista area in Rio Grande County.  A modular building was flipped on its top by the wind in Pueblo West. A few trees were also uprooted in the City of Pueblo.  Winds caused power lines to come down in Manitou Springs, which sparked several small grass fires near the Pikes Peak Cog Railway.  Three hangers at Peterson Air Force Base were evacuated, and some minor roof damage occurred.  Some trees were uprooted in the greater Colorado Springs area.  On Fort Carson, eight soldiers were injured when a tent they were setting up blew over.  One soldier suffered a broken leg.

A few trees were also uprooted in the City of Pueblo.  Winds caused power lines to come down in Manitou Springs, which sparked several small grass fires near the Pikes Peak Cog Railway.  Three hangers at Peterson Air Force Base were evacuated, and some minor roof damage occurred.  Some trees were uprooted in the greater Colorado Springs area.  On Fort Carson, eight soldiers were injured when a tent they were setting up blew over.  One soldier suffered a broken leg.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail, ranging in size from quarters to baseballs.  The hail reportedly piled up to 8 inches deep in Elbert County where the largest hail was observed.  Extensive damage was reported to vehicles near Interstate 70 and State Highway 86.  Intense thunderstorm winds damaged trees and building in Genoa.  Heavy rain produced flash flooding in parts of Elbert and Larimer Counties.  One tornado touched down near Karval.  It reportedly uprooted several trees and destroyed a garage.
